sections.label: Comprehension
sections.content:
**Overview**
The comprehension section tests your ability to read, understand, and analyse a passage. You will be given a prose text (approximately 500–700 words) and asked questions testing literal, inferential, and evaluative understanding.

**Key Skills**
- Identify the main idea and purpose of the text
- Determine the tone and mood (e.g., formal, ironic, nostalgic, critical)
- Recognise bias and point of view
- Understand connotation and denotation of key words
- Interpret figurative language and rhetorical devices
- Draw inferences and make predictions
- Evaluate the effectiveness of the writer's choices

**Exam Tips**
- Read the questions BEFORE reading the passage — this focuses your attention
- Skim the passage first, then read carefully
- Quote accurately from the text when asked; use quotation marks
- For 'in your own words' questions, DO NOT copy from the passage
- Pay attention to mark allocation — 2 marks = 2 points
- Time: spend approximately 30–35 minutes on this section

**Common Question Types**
- *Literal questions*: answers are directly in the text
- *Inferential questions*: read between the lines
- *Evaluative questions*: your opinion supported by evidence from the text

sections.label: Summary
sections.content:
**Overview**
The summary tests your ability to condense a text into seven main points, using approximately 90 words. You must identify the key ideas and express them concisely in your own words.

**Key Rules**
- Write in full sentences (not point form unless instructed otherwise)
- Use your OWN words — paraphrasing is essential
- Include ONLY the main ideas; leave out examples, quotes, and repetition
- Stick to the word limit (approximately 90 words)
- Indicate the word count at the end
- Write in a single连贯 paragraph unless told otherwise

**How to Summarise**
1. Read the passage carefully to understand the overall meaning
2. Underline or highlight the SEVEN main points
3. Number each point to ensure you have seven
4. Paraphrase each point in your own words
5. Link the points into a coherent paragraph
6. Count your words and write the total in brackets

**Common Mistakes**
- Copying directly from the text (no marks for direct quotation)
- Including too many details or examples
- Exceeding the word limit
- Writing in point form when paragraphs are required
- Missing one or more of the seven points

**Marking**
- 7 marks for content (1 mark per point)
- 3 marks for language and style (conciseness, paraphrasing, grammar)
- Total: 10 marks

sections.label: Visual Literacy
sections.content:
**Overview**
Visual literacy tests your ability to interpret and analyse visual texts — typically advertisements and cartoons.

**Analysing Advertisements**
- Identify the *product* or *service* being advertised
- Determine the *target audience* (age, gender, social class, interests)
- Analyse the *layout*: placement of images, text, logo, slogan
- Examine *colour* choices and their emotional associations
- Identify *persuasive techniques*: bandwagon, testimonial, emotional appeal, logical appeal, fear appeal, humour
- Analyse *font* choices: bold, italic, size, style
- Comment on the *headline*, *body copy*, and *slogan*
- Evaluate the overall *effectiveness* of the advertisement

**Analysing Cartoons**
- Identify the *context* and *setting* of the cartoon
- Analyse *characters*: facial expressions, body language, posture
- Examine *speech/thought bubbles* and what they reveal
- Identify *caricature* and *stereotype* (exaggerated features for effect)
- Analyse *humour techniques*: irony, sarcasm, pun, exaggeration, understatement
- Comment on the *message* or *social commentary*
- Evaluate the *effectiveness* of the cartoonist's choices

**Key Terminology**
- Font, bold, italics, size, capitalisation
- Foreground, background, focal point
- Salience (what stands out)
- Gaze (direct address vs indirect)
- Angle (high angle = powerlessness, low angle = power)
- Lighting, shadow, contrast

sections.label: Language Structures
sections.content:
**Overview**
This section tests your knowledge of grammar, vocabulary, and language conventions. Questions are contextual — you analyse language use within a given passage.

**Key Topics**
- **Parts of speech**: noun, pronoun, verb, adjective, adverb, preposition, conjunction, interjection
- **Sentence types**: simple, compound, complex, compound-complex
- **Sentence functions**: declarative, interrogative, imperative, exclamatory
- **Tense**: present, past, future; simple, continuous, perfect, perfect continuous
- **Voice**: active and passive (know when each is used)
- **Direct and indirect (reported) speech**: rules for conversion
- **Concord**: subject-verb agreement (singular/plural)
- **Punctuation**: full stop, comma, colon, semicolon, dash, hyphen, apostrophe, quotation marks, ellipsis
- **Word formation**: prefixes, suffixes, roots, compounds
- **Synonyms and antonyms**: words with similar/opposite meanings
- **Figures of speech**: simile, metaphor, personification, hyperbole, litotes, irony, sarcasm, oxymoron, paradox, alliteration, assonance, onomatopoeia

**Exam Tips**
- Read the question carefully — if it asks for ONE word, give ONE word
- For 'correct the error' questions, rewrite the entire sentence correctly
- Know the difference between formal and informal language
- Understand denotation (dictionary meaning) vs connotation (emotional association)
- For figure of speech identification: look for the technique, not just the effect

sections.label: Critical Language Awareness
sections.content:
**Overview**
Critical Language Awareness (CLA) tests your ability to see how language is used to influence, manipulate, or persuade. You analyse the hidden messages, biases, and power dynamics in texts.

**Key Concepts**
- **Bias**: favouring one side unfairly; recognise one-sided presentation of facts
- **Stereotyping**: generalising about a group of people (race, gender, age, class)
- **Emotive language**: words chosen to provoke an emotional response (e.g., 'slaughter' vs 'cull')
- **Loaded language**: words with strong positive or negative connotations
- **Euphemism**: mild expression substituted for a harsh one (e.g., 'passed away' for 'died')
- **Dysphemism**: harsh expression for effect (e.g., 'kicked the bucket')
- **Propaganda techniques**: name-calling, glittering generalities, transfer, testimonial, plain folks, bandwagon, fear
- **Manipulation**: deliberate distortion or omission of facts to influence readers
- **Power relations**: who has power in the text and how is it expressed?
- **Register**: formal vs informal language and why it's used

**Questions to Ask**
- Who created this text? For what purpose?
- Who is the target audience?
- What is omitted or silenced?
- Whose interests are served by this text?
- How does word choice reveal attitude?
- What assumptions does the text make?

**Exam Tips**
- Always support your answer with evidence from the text
- Identify the specific words/phrases that reveal bias or manipulation
- Consider the broader social and political context
- Be aware of your own biases when analysing texts

sections.label: Poetry: General Approach
sections.content:
**Overview**
Paper 2 Section A tests your knowledge of prescribed poetry (12 poems) and unseen poetry. You must answer:
- TWO prescribed poetry questions (one essay + one contextual, or two contextuals)
- ONE unseen poetry question (compulsory)

**How to Analyse a Poem**
1. **First reading**: Read for overall meaning and emotional impact
2. **Speaker and situation**: Who is speaking? To whom? What is happening?
3. **Theme**: What is the poem about? (love, death, nature, identity, politics, etc.)
4. **Structure**: Stanzas, rhyme scheme, metre, line length, enjambment, caesura
5. **Diction**: Word choice — formal/informal, concrete/abstract, positive/negative connotations
6. **Imagery**: What pictures does the poem create? Sensory details (visual, auditory, tactile)
7. **Figures of speech**: Metaphor, simile, personification, symbol, irony, paradox
8. **Sound devices**: Alliteration, assonance, onomatopoeia, rhythm
9. **Tone and mood**: What is the speaker's attitude? How does the poem make you feel?
10. **Conclusion**: What is the poem's overall message? How effective is it?

**Prescribed Poems (Imagined Worlds Anthology)**

a) Sonnet 130 – William Shakespeare
b) The child who was shot dead by soldiers in Nyanga – Ingrid Jonker
c) Ata Funeral – Dennis Brutus
d) Poem of Return – Jofre Rocha
e) Talk to the Peach Tree – Sipho Sepamla
f) Prayer to Masks – Léopold Sédar Senghor
g) This Winter Coming – Karen Press
h) Solitude – Ella Wheeler Wilcox
i) The Morning Sun is Shining – Olive Schreiner
j) It is a beauteous evening, calm and free – William Wordsworth
k) Fern Hill – Dylan Thomas
l) The Shipwreck – Emily Dickinson

sections.label: Novel: Life of Pi – Yann Martel
sections.content:
**Overview**
*Life of Pi* tells the story of Piscine Molitor Patel (Pi), a young Indian boy who survives a shipwreck and spends 227 days adrift in the Pacific Ocean on a lifeboat with a Bengal tiger named Richard Parker.

**Key Characters**
- **Pi Patel**: The protagonist — curious, spiritual, resilient. Practices Hinduism, Christianity, and Islam simultaneously. His name derives from a swimming pool in Paris ('Piscine').
- **Richard Parker**: A 450-pound Bengal tiger. Pi's unlikely companion on the lifeboat. Named after a character in a novel.
- **Santosh Patel**: Pi's father — rational, practical, runs the Pondicherry Zoo.
- **Gita Patel**: Pi's mother — protective, loving.
- **Ravi Patel**: Pi's older brother — teasing but caring.

**Key Themes**
- **Faith and religion**: Pi practises multiple faiths; the novel explores the nature of belief
- **Survival**: Physical and psychological survival against impossible odds
- **Storytelling**: The novel questions which story is true and why we choose to believe
- **Identity**: Pi's journey of self-discovery
- **The relationship between humans and animals**: Pi learns from Richard Parker

**Key Symbols**
- The **lifeboat**: a microcosm of the world; a space of survival and confrontation
- **Richard Parker**: represents Pi's own instinct for survival and primal nature
- The **island** (with cannibalistic trees): illusion of safety; temptation of stagnation
- **Orange juice**: the orangutan — maternal protection and loss
- **Water**: both life-giving and threatening

**Plot Summary**
1. Pi's childhood in Pondicherry, India — the zoo, religious exploration
2. Family decides to move to Canada, taking animals with them
3. The ship sinks (Tsimtsum) — Pi finds himself on a lifeboat with a zebra, hyena, orangutan, and Richard Parker
4. The animals fight — only Pi and Richard Parker remain
5. Pi and Richard Parker learn to coexist on the lifeboat
6. They arrive at the mysterious floating island
7. They reach the coast of Mexico — Richard Parker disappears into the jungle
8. Pi is interviewed by Japanese officials; tells them two versions of the story

**Exam Tips**
- For the **essay**: develop a clear argument with evidence from the text
- For the **contextual question**: read the extract carefully before answering
- Know key quotes for each major character and theme
- Understand the novel's frame narrative structure (Pi telling his story to a writer)

sections.label: Novel: The Picture of Dorian Gray – Oscar Wilde
sections.content:
**Overview**
*The Picture of Dorian Gray* follows a handsome young man who wishes to remain youthful forever. His portrait ages and bears the marks of his sins while he stays physically untouched.

**Key Characters**
- **Dorian Gray**: A beautiful young man who becomes corrupted by vanity and hedonism
- **Lord Henry Wotton**: An aristocratic hedonist who influences Dorian with his cynical philosophy
- **Basil Hallward**: The artist who paints Dorian's portrait; he is morally upright and deeply affected by Dorian's corruption
- **Sybil Vane**: A young actress loved by Dorian; her suicide marks the beginning of his moral descent
- **James Vane**: Sybil's brother — seeks revenge against Dorian
- **Alan Campbell**: A chemist whom Dorian blackmails

**Key Themes**
- **Vanity and youth**: The obsession with physical beauty and eternal youth
- **Corruption and morality**: The consequences of living without moral restraint
- **Art and life**: The relationship between art and reality; the portrait as a reflection of the soul
- **Influence and manipulation**: Lord Henry's corrupting influence on Dorian
- **Secrets and hypocrisy**: Dorian's double life — public respectability vs private depravity

**Key Symbols**
- The **portrait**: Dorian's conscience and the physical manifestation of his sins
- The **yellow book**: Lord Henry gives Dorian a decadent French novel that influences his lifestyle
- The **opium dens**: Dorian's escape from reality; the depths of his corruption
- **Dorian's beauty**: A mask for his inner corruption

**Plot Summary**
1. Basil paints Dorian's portrait; Dorian wishes it would age instead of him
2. Lord Henry influences Dorian with his philosophy of hedonism
3. Dorian falls in love with Sybil Vane; when her performance fails, he cruelly rejects her
4. Sybil commits suicide; Dorian notices the portrait has changed — a cruel expression appears
5. Dorian embarks on a life of pleasure and sin; the portrait grows increasingly hideous
6. Dorian shows Basil the portrait and murders him
7. Dorian blackmails Alan Campbell to dispose of Basil's body
8. James Vane pursues Dorian for revenge; dies in a hunting accident
9. Dorian decides to reform but the portrait remains unchanged
10. In a rage, Dorian stabs the portrait; he dies and the portrait returns to its original state

**Exam Tips**
- The novel is told in third-person limited narration
- Wilde's style is epigrammatic (full of witty sayings)
- Know the Preface — it outlines Wilde's philosophy of art
- For contextual questions: pay attention to what happens just before and after the extract

sections.label: Drama: Hamlet – William Shakespeare
sections.content:
**Overview**
*Hamlet* is Shakespeare's tragedy of revenge. Prince Hamlet of Denmark is visited by his father's ghost, who reveals he was murdered by his own brother, Claudius — who has now married Hamlet's mother, Gertrude, and taken the throne.

**Key Characters**
- **Hamlet**: Prince of Denmark — intellectual, melancholic, contemplative, feigns madness
- **Claudius**: King of Denmark, Hamlet's uncle — ambitious, guilty, manipulative
- **Gertrude**: Queen of Denmark, Hamlet's mother — loving but weak
- **Ophelia**: Polonius's daughter, Hamlet's love interest — driven to madness and death by drowning
- **Polonius**: Lord Chamberlain — pompous, nosy, gives long-winded advice
- **Laertes**: Polonius's son — passionate, seeks revenge for his father's and sister's deaths
- **Horatio**: Hamlet's loyal friend — the voice of reason
- **The Ghost**: Hamlet's father — demands revenge

**Key Themes**
- **Revenge**: The central conflict — is revenge justified? Should it be immediate or considered?
- **Madness**: Is Hamlet truly mad or feigning? Ophelia's madness is real
- **Mortality**: The famous 'To be or not to be' soliloquy; the graveyard scene
- **Deception**: 'To thine own self be true' — everyone is deceiving or being deceived
- **Action vs inaction**: Hamlet's tragic flaw (procrastination)

**Key Soliloquies**
- "O, that this too, too solid flesh would melt" (Act 1, Scene 2)
- "O, what a rogue and peasant slave am I" (Act 2, Scene 2)
- "To be, or not to be" (Act 3, Scene 1)
- "How all occasions do inform against me" (Act 4, Scene 4)

**Plot Summary**
1. The Ghost reveals Claudius's treachery and commands Hamlet to avenge him
2. Hamlet feigns madness to investigate
3. The "play within a play" (The Mousetrap) confirms Claudius's guilt
4. Hamlet confronts Gertrude; kills Polonius behind the arras
5. Claudius sends Hamlet to England (to be killed)
6. Ophelia drowns (possibly suicide)
7. Laertes returns seeking revenge
8. Claudius plots with Laertes to kill Hamlet in a fencing match
9. Gertrude drinks poison, Laertes and Hamlet are wounded with the poisoned sword
10. Hamlet kills Claudius before dying; Fortinbras takes the throne

**Exam Tips**
- For contextual questions: analyse language, imagery, and dramatic effect
- Understand Elizabethan conventions (revenge tragedy, soliloquy, dramatic irony)
- Know the key quotes for each character

sections.label: Drama: Othello – William Shakespeare
sections.content:
**Overview**
*Othello* is Shakespeare's tragedy of jealousy and manipulation. Othello, a Moorish general in the Venetian army, is driven to murder his wife Desdemona by the machinations of his ensign, Iago.

**Key Characters**
- **Othello**: The protagonist — noble, respected general; becomes consumed by jealousy
- **Desdemona**: Othello's wife — virtuous, loyal, loving; tragically murdered
- **Iago**: The villain — Othello's ensign; manipulates everyone out of resentment and ambition
- **Cassio**: Othello's lieutenant — honourable but vulnerable to Iago's schemes
- **Emilia**: Iago's wife, Desdemona's attendant — discovers the truth too late
- **Roderigo**: A Venetian gentleman — in love with Desdemona, used by Iago
- **Brabantio**: Desdemona's father — accuses Othello of witchcraft

**Key Themes**
- **Jealousy**: The 'green-eyed monster' — Othello's jealousy is exploited by Iago
- **Race and otherness**: Othello is an outsider in Venetian society
- **Manipulation**: Iago's skill at appearing honest while destroying everyone
- **Appearance vs reality**: 'I am not what I am' (Iago)
- **Honour and reputation**: Cassio's concern for his reputation; Othello's military honour

**Key Symbols**
- The **handkerchief**: Othello's first gift to Desdemona; becomes 'proof' of her infidelity
- The **candle**: In the murder scene — Desdemona's life is like a candle that Othello extinguishes
- **Animals and monsters**: Iago uses animal imagery to dehumanise Othello and Desdemona

**Plot Summary**
1. Iago is furious that Cassio was promoted over him
2. Iago plots to ruin Othello by making him believe Desdemona is unfaithful
3. Iago gets Cassio drunk and disgraced; advises him to ask Desdemona to plead his case
4. Iago manipulates Othello into suspecting Desdemona and Cassio
5. Emilia gives Iago the handkerchief; Iago plants it on Cassio
6. Othello becomes convinced of Desdemona's guilt
7. Othello kills Desdemona smothering her in their bed
8. Emilia reveals the truth; Iago kills her and flees
9. Othello realises his terrible mistake and kills himself
10. Cassio becomes governor; Iago is taken away for punishment

**Exam Tips**
- Iago's soliloquies reveal his true intentions (dramatic irony)
- The play is set in Cyprus and Venice — contrast between civilised and military worlds
- Othello is a domestic tragedy as much as it is about race and society

sections.label: Drama: The Crucible – Arthur Miller
sections.content:
**Overview**
*The Crucible* is Arthur Miller's allegorical play about the Salem witch trials of 1692, written as a critique of the McCarthy-era anti-communist 'witch hunts' of the 1950s.

**Key Characters**
- **John Proctor**: The protagonist — a farmer, honest but guilty of adultery; dies rather than sign a false confession
- **Elizabeth Proctor**: John's wife — morally upright, cold, forgiving
- **Abigail Williams**: The antagonist — a teenage girl who manipulates the witch trials to get revenge on Elizabeth and win John
- **Reverend Parris**: Salem's minister — paranoid, self-serving, concerned with his reputation
- **Reverend Hale**: A witchcraft expert — arrives confident, leaves disillusioned
- **Deputy Governor Danforth**: The judge — obsessed with the court's authority, refuses to admit mistakes
- **Mary Warren**: Proctor's servant — weak, torn between truth and fear
- **Judge Hathorne**: A harsh, unyielding magistrate

**Key Themes**
- **Hysteria**: How fear and panic spread through a community
- **Reputation**: The driving force for many characters (Parris, Proctor, Danforth)
- **Authority and justice**: The abuse of power in the name of justice
- **Truth and confession**: Proctor's final choice — truth with integrity, or false confession
- **Allegory**: The play criticises McCarthyism (accusations without evidence, blacklisting)

**Key Symbols**
- The **crucible**: A severe test or trial (literally a container for melting metal) — Proctor is 'tested' and purified through his ordeal
- The **court**: Represents corrupted authority
- The **poppet (doll)**: Used as 'evidence' to accuse Elizabeth of witchcraft
- **The forest**: Where the girls were caught dancing — the wild, unknown

**Plot Summary**
1. Girls are caught dancing in the forest; Reverend Parris fears scandal
2. Abigail threatens the girls to keep silent
3. Betty Parris and Ruth Putnam are 'afflicted' — rumour of witchcraft spreads
4. Reverend Hale arrives to investigate
5. Abigail accuses Tituba, then others — the hysteria begins
6. John Proctor confronts Abigail about her lies
7. Mary Warren gives Elizabeth a poppet — Abigail uses it to frame Elizabeth
8. Elizabeth is arrested; Proctor vows to expose Abigail
9. Proctor brings Mary Warren to testify; Mary breaks down and accuses Proctor
10. Proctor is arrested; Hale quits the court in disgust
11. Proctor is offered a choice: confess (live with a lie) or hang (die with integrity)
12. Proctor signs the confession but tears it up; he chooses to die with his name intact

**Exam Tips**
- The play is an allegory for the McCarthy 'Red Scare' — know the historical parallel
- Acts are divided by setting (the Proctor home vs the court)
- Key quote: 'Because it is my name! Because I cannot have another in my life!' — Proctor's choice

sections.label: Unseen Poetry
sections.content:
**Overview**
The unseen poetry section presents a poem you have never studied. You must analyse it on the spot. This is compulsory — you cannot choose a different question.

**Step-by-Step Approach**
1. **Read the poem TWICE**: First for general meaning, second for detail
2. **Identify the speaker and situation**: Who is speaking? What is happening?
3. **Determine the tone**: Is the speaker joyful, sorrowful, angry, reflective, ironic?
4. **Identify the theme**: What is the poem about? (love, loss, nature, protest, identity)
5. **Analyse structure**: Stanzas, rhyme scheme, repetition, line length, enjambment
6. **Highlight key words**: Look for powerful or unusual word choices
7. **Identify figures of speech**: Metaphor, simile, personification, etc.
8. **Analyse sound devices**: Alliteration, assonance, onomatopoeia
9. **Consider the title and final lines**: Often contain the poem's central message
10. **Formulate your response**: Answer the question directly with evidence from the poem

**Common Question Types**
- *What is the poem about?* (theme)
- *Comment on the poet's use of imagery* (provide examples)
- *Discuss the tone of the poem* (with evidence)
- *Explain the effectiveness of a specific device*
- *Refer to the structure* (how does it support meaning?)

**Key Terminology to Use**
- Stanza, line, couplet, quatrain, sonnet
- Rhyme, half-rhyme, internal rhyme
- Enjambment, caesura, end-stopped line
- Alliteration, assonance, consonance
- Metaphor, simile, personification, symbol
- Irony, paradox, oxymoron, hyperbole
- Tone, mood, atmosphere
- Imagery (visual, auditory, tactile, olfactory)

**Exam Tips**
- Don't panic — the poem is unfamiliar to everyone
- Trust your instincts; your first impression is often correct
- Support every point with a quote from the poem
- If you don't know a word, use context clues to guess its meaning
- Stay calm and systematic

sections.label: Essay Writing
sections.content:
**Overview**
Paper 3 Section A requires you to write one essay of 400–450 words. You choose from a range of topics covering different essay types.

**Essay Types**
1. **Narrative**: Tells a story — use vivid description, sensory details, dialogue, a clear plot structure (beginning, middle, end)
2. **Descriptive**: Paints a picture with words — focus on sensory details (sight, sound, smell, touch, taste), figurative language, atmosphere
3. **Reflective**: Explores personal thoughts and feelings — informal, thoughtful, may use flashback, often philosophical
4. **Discursive**: Presents a balanced argument on a topic — discusses both sides before reaching a conclusion; formal, logical
5. **Argumentative**: Persuades the reader to agree with a particular point of view — clear stance, strong evidence, formal tone

**Structure**
1. **Introduction** (40–50 words): Hook the reader, introduce the topic, state your approach/thesis
2. **Body** (300–350 words, 2–3 paragraphs): Develop your ideas with evidence, examples, sensory detail
3. **Conclusion** (30–40 words): Bring the essay to a satisfying close, reflect on the main idea

**Marking Rubric**
- **Content and planning (25 marks)**: Originality, depth, coherence
- **Language and style (15 marks)**: Vocabulary, sentence structure, tone, register
- **Structure (10 marks)**: Logical flow, paragraphing, transitions

**Exam Tips**
- Spend 5–10 minutes **planning** before you write
- Write a **mind map** or outline
- Use vivid vocabulary — avoid overused words (nice, good, bad, nice)
- Vary sentence length for effect
- Use figurative language (especially for descriptive/reflective)
- Proofread at the end for spelling and grammar errors
- Stay within the word limit (400–450 words)
- Write NEATLY and legibly

sections.label: Longer Transactional Texts
sections.content:
**Overview**
Paper 3 Section B requires you to write one longer transactional text of 180–200 words. The register and format depend on the text type.

**Common Text Types**

**Formal Letter**
- Sender's address, date, recipient's address
- Salutation (Dear Sir/Madam or Dear Mr/Ms Smith)
- Subject line (in bold or underlined)
- Formal register, polite tone
- Paragraph 1: reason for writing
- Middle paragraphs: details
- Final paragraph: action expected
- Subscription (Yours faithfully / Yours sincerely)
- Signature and printed name

**Speech**
- Salutation (Good morning, fellow students...)
- Engaging opening (rhetorical question, anecdote)
- Clear structure: introduction, points, conclusion
- Rhetorical devices for effect
- Inclusive language ('we', 'us')
- Call to action in conclusion

**Newspaper Article**
- Headline (eye-catching, informative)
- Byline (By John Smith)
- Lead paragraph: who, what, when, where, why
- Inverted pyramid style (most important info first)
- Quotes from witnesses/experts
- Objective tone (usually)

**Magazine Feature Article**
- Catchy headline and sub-heading
- Byline
- More creative and descriptive than a newspaper article
- May include quotes, anecdotes, descriptive passages
- Less formal tone than a news report

**CV (Curriculum Vitae)**
- Personal details
- Education (most recent first)
- Work experience
- Skills
- References
- Formal and concise

**Interview (as Questioner)**
- Introduction of interviewee
- Questions (usually with answers paraphrased or quoted)
- Concluding observations
- Could be written as Q&A format or as an article

**General Tips**
- Know the format conventions for EACH text type
- Use the CORRECT register (formal vs informal)
- Stay within the word limit
- Read the question carefully — it will specify the audience and purpose

sections.label: Shorter Transactional Texts
sections.content:
**Overview**
Paper 3 Section C requires you to write one shorter transactional text of 100–120 words. Though shorter, these still require correct format and register.

**Common Text Types**

**Diary Entry**
- Date at the top
- Informal, personal register
- First person (I)
- Express emotions and personal reflections
- May include colloquial language
- Dated entries (one or more)

**Email**
- To:, From:, Subject: fields
- Salutation
- Body (concise)
- Subscription
- Register depends on recipient (friend vs boss)

**Postcard**
- Brief, informal
- Written from a holiday or event
- Address format on one side
- Informal greetings
- Limited space — get to the point

**Dialogue**
- Character names in capitals or bold
- No quotation marks
- Natural speech patterns
- Reveal character through language
- Include stage directions if needed (in brackets)

**Written Advertisement**
- Eye-catching headline
- Persuasive language
- Details of product/service
- Contact information
- Slogan
- Visual description (if relevant)

**Notice**
- Heading (NOTICE)
- Date
- Clear, concise information
- Who, what, when, where
- Contact person/details
- Formal register

**General Tips**
- Every word counts — no waffling
- Check format carefully (each text type has specific conventions)
- Use appropriate register (formal/informal)
- Proofread for spelling and punctuation errors
- Stay within the word limit — marks are deducted for being too short or too long
- Practise writing each text type before the exam
